prompt_md: Why should you use `std::chrono::steady_clock` instead of `std::chrono::system_clock` when measuring elapsed time?

options.a: `steady_clock` is monotonically increasing and never goes backward; `system_clock` can be adjusted by NTP or the user, making elapsed-time measurements unreliable.
options.b: `system_clock` is only available on Windows, while `steady_clock` is cross-platform.
options.c: `steady_clock` has nanosecond resolution while `system_clock` only has millisecond resolution.

explanation_md: `steady_clock` guarantees monotonic progression — it never goes backward — making it safe for measuring elapsed durations. `system_clock` reflects wall-clock calendar time and can jump forward or backward if the system clock is adjusted, producing negative or grossly wrong elapsed times.

options.a: `elapsed` is a duration object; `.count()` must be called to extract the integer value before printing.
options.b: `duration_cast` is not part of `<chrono>`; you must include `<duration>` separately.
options.c: `end - start` is not valid; you must use `std::chrono::subtract(end, start)` instead.
options.d: `auto` cannot deduce the type of `start` and `end`; you must write the full type explicitly.

explanation_md: `std::chrono::duration_cast` returns a duration object, not an integer. To print the numeric count of milliseconds you must call `elapsed.count()`. In C++20 streaming a duration object directly is allowed, but in C++17 and earlier it does not compile without `.count()`.

prompt_md: You time a function that runs in 800 microseconds using `std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>`. What value does `.count()` return?

options.a: 0 — duration_cast truncates toward zero, and 800 microseconds is less than one millisecond.
options.b: 1 — duration_cast rounds to the nearest millisecond.
options.c: 800 — the value is preserved regardless of the cast target.
options.d: 0.8 — fractional milliseconds are stored as a floating-point value.

explanation_md: `duration_cast` truncates (not rounds) toward zero. 800 microseconds is 0.8 milliseconds, which truncates to 0. To measure 800-microsecond events accurately, cast to `std::chrono::microseconds` instead, which will give `.count()` == 800.

The loop computes 0+1+2+...+99 = 4950, so `Result: 4950` is printed first. The loop itself is so fast (far under 1 ms) that `duration_cast<milliseconds>` truncates to 0, printing `Time: 0 ms`. The exact time value is machine-dependent but will almost certainly be 0 ms for 100 iterations.
